description: FIG. 1. (Color online) Electrodeposited Cu with high-density growth twins: (a) TEM micrograph of the as-deposited microstructure, (b) length scale (spacing) distribution determined from TEM micrographs for the direction perpendicular to the TBs, and (c) TEM micrograph after $77\mathrm{K}$ straining to $\sim 30\%$ , showing the accumulation of dislocations.

FIG. 2. (Color online) (a) Engineering tensile stress-strain curves of nanotwinned Cu tested at different strain rates and temperatures, in comparison with that of an UFG Cu tested at $77\mathrm{K}$ , (b) true $\sigma - \varepsilon$ curves, (c) the normalized work hardening rate at $77\mathrm{K}$ , $\Theta = 1 / \sigma (\partial \sigma /\partial \varepsilon)_{\varepsilon}$ , where $\sigma$ is true stress and $\varepsilon$ true strain. At $\varepsilon = 1\%$ , $\Theta = 20$ and 5 for nano-twinned and UFG Cu, respectively (and $\Theta = 11$ and 1 at $\varepsilon = 2\%$ ). Necking sets in when $\Theta \leqslant 1$ , such that for UFG Cu the true strain and $\Theta$ cannot be evaluated after $\varepsilon = 2\%$ . Coarse-grained Cu is also included for comparison. The inset illustrates the sustained $\Theta$ at high stresses, in contrast with coarse-grained Cu.

FIG. 3. TEM micrograph showing the deposition of Shockley (short arrows) and Frank (T) partial dislocations at twin boundaries, after RT deformation of nano-twinned Cu. Also included is a dissociation reaction of a unit dislocation into Frank and Shockley partials: $\frac{1}{2}[10\overline{1}]$ (black dashed arrow) $\rightarrow \frac{1}{3}[1\overline{1}\overline{1}] + \frac{1}{6}[12\overline{1}]$ .
